scenarios.title: /summary binds startTime and endTime as nullable DateTimeOffset query parameters
scenarios.rationale: Switching from DateTime? to DateTimeOffset? is a wire contract change. Consumers that previously sent unzoned ISO-8601 must now include an offset (or 'Z'). The new binding behaviour is the foundation of every downstream date check.

scenarios.title: Requesting a window > 60 days returns 400 BadRequest with an actionable error
scenarios.rationale: MaxStrictDateRangeDays = 60 is the new contract-side floor on query cost. Window-too-large is the single most common failure mode for the new contract and the controller must reject with a 400 carrying the limit value, not silently clamp.

scenarios.title: startTime older than MaxStrictDateRangeDays returns 400 BadRequest
scenarios.rationale: Even if the window is within 60 days, an absolute-older startTime is rejected. This anchors the SQL query plan to a bounded retention horizon and prevents accidental full-table scans against the historic partition.

scenarios.title: endTime later than UtcNow returns 400 BadRequest
scenarios.rationale: A future endTime would either return zero rows or trip an off-by-one in the range comparison. The controller must reject explicitly so callers correct the request rather than silently get an empty payload.

scenarios.title: endTime less than or equal to startTime returns 400 BadRequest
scenarios.rationale: Range inversion is a contract violation that produces empty / nonsensical aggregates downstream. ResolveStrictDateRange must catch it at the boundary and emit a clear error.

scenarios.title: /trends endpoint resolves its date window through the same ResolveStrictDateRange helper as /summary
scenarios.rationale: The three endpoints (/summary, /trends, /topErrors) must enforce identical date semantics. If /trends used a different helper or branched on its own bounds, the dashboard could show inconsistent inclusion/exclusion across panels for the same user-selected window.

scenarios.title: /topErrors endpoint resolves its date window through ResolveStrictDateRange
scenarios.rationale: Same parity argument as /trends — the third endpoint must use the shared helper. Any drift here is a contract-divergence bug that's invisible until a user crosses a 60-day boundary on /topErrors but not /summary.

scenarios.title: /summary endpoint resolves its date window through ResolveStrictDateRange before issuing the SQL
scenarios.rationale: The /summary call site is the canonical example for the new helper. Verifying the call site (not just the helper) catches a regression where someone bypasses the helper to re-introduce DateTime parsing.

scenarios.title: ResolveStrictDateRange anchors its checks against DateTimeOffset.UtcNow, not Server local time
scenarios.rationale: The helper uses DateTimeOffset.UtcNow as 'now'. If a refactor swapped in DateTime.Now (server local), the 60-day window check would shift by the host's timezone offset — silently miscounting boundary days for non-UTC deployments.

scenarios.title: MaxStrictDateRangeDays compile-time constant is 60
scenarios.rationale: The constant is the single source of truth for the date-window cap. Changing it (even by one) shifts the contract and changes the SQL query plan cost — the constant value itself is a wire-visible promise to callers.
